summary: DHH reflects on the delayed emotional processing of major life changes, noting how the subconscious lags behind conscious awareness. Using his girlfriend's departure to Hong Kong as an example, he observes that it took a month for the emotional reality to set in. He extends this observation to his own recent departure from full-time employment, wondering when that change will emotionally register. He frames humans as creatures of pattern who resist updating their self-image.
key_points: Major life changes understate their significance at the moment they occur
The subconscious delays appropriate emotional responses through a bureaucratic-like internal process
Humans are pattern creatures whose self-image updates slowly and reluctantly
DHH has just left full-time employment and is curious how that shift will eventually feel
money_quotes: It's funny how major changes in your life often seems to under-state their significance at the time of occurrence.
Getting access to change the self-image appears to be a long and bureaucratic process.
Why is it necessary for your subconscious to hold back the appropriate emotions?
We're a species of pattern creatures.
key_insight: Humans are pattern creatures whose emotional processing of major life changes lags far behind the events themselves, as if the subconscious runs its own slow bureaucracy for updating self-image.
